directions.name: Register-first PTX export follow-through on the hot band
directions.hypothesis: The PTX branch has now delivered three consecutive wins, and round 3 specifically showed that orchestration/barrier cleanup is paying off: runtime is down to 33.366 ms, barrier is 14.34, and mio is only 2.31. The hot 64x384 kernel still exits through the shared export surface at src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:301-315, :405-439, and :894-896, which means the branch still materializes every accumulator tile through shared before BF16 packing. With control/orchestration already improved, the best next step is to make the hot-band PTX branch own export as well: retire accumulator tiles from registers more directly so shared LSU work, short scoreboard, and export-side live state all shrink while leaving the 64x96 tail untouched.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The remaining cost has shifted away from barrier and mio tax toward export-side shared/LSU work and residual register lifetime in the hot-band epilogue.
directions.risk: Direct register export changes accumulator-to-output mapping and BF16 packing at the same time, so correctness risk is real. It also may not flip occupancy_limit_registers by itself if the dominant register cost is still the 12-tile accumulator residency.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if export-side shared/LSU pressure does not fall materially or if registers increase enough to offset any store-path gain.

directions.name: Full-width explicit PTX load-order control without the regressed half-panel path
directions.hypothesis: The hot-band PTX kernel still uses PTX wrappers around wmma.load and wmma.mma at src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:261-299 and :746-763. A coherent next step inside the same branch is to move to more explicit compute/load-order control for the full 64x384 tile set, but not by reopening the prechecked explicit mma.sync half-panel subpath that already regressed. The safer variant is a full-width PTX dataflow follow-through: keep the 12-tile hot band intact, keep the 64x96 tail unchanged, and use more explicit fragment load order to attack the now-rising memory/latency side of the kernel as compute-memory and DRAM throughput have climbed into the mid-50% range.
directions.expected_bottleneck: With barrier and mio now low, residual long-scoreboard and feed latency inside the PTX compute body are a more plausible limiter than orchestration tax.
directions.risk: Explicit fragment load control is a larger rewrite and can easily recreate the wrong lessons from the rejected half-panel mma.sync precheck if it starts splitting the output tile the same way. It can also raise registers or instruction count if the full-width mapping is not disciplined.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the implementation starts collapsing into the old half-panel explicit mma.sync experiment or if long-scoreboard and runtime do not improve together.

directions.name: PTX helper and lifetime compaction for the 12-tile accumulator set
directions.hypothesis: Round 2's switch to named accumulator tiles clearly helped, but the branch is still at 167 registers/thread and one block/SM. A narrower next step inside the same PTX line is to keep the current arithmetic path and tail unchanged while compacting helper structure and lifetime: reduce recursion and oversized helper scope in ptx_wmma_acc_tile(), ptx_wmma_accumulate_tile_set_384(), and ptx_wmma_store_tile_set_384() so the compiler has a better chance of tightening live ranges around the 12-tile hot set without reopening the rejected half-panel compute split.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Compiler-visible live-range inflation around the named 12-tile PTX helper surface is still contributing to the occupancy guardrail even after orchestration improvements.
directions.risk: This is the lowest-ceiling option: it may shave a few registers but not enough to change occupancy_limit_registers, and it could accidentally undo the favorable compile-time scheduling that made round 2 and round 3 work.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if helper/lifetime compaction does not move registers or active warps at all, because then the branch should stay focused on export or full-width PTX dataflow instead.

notes: All three directions stay strictly inside the 64x384 hot-band PTX microkernel branch and keep the 64x96 tail unchanged. Ranking uses the new fact that round 3 pushed runtime down again to 33.366 ms while barrier and mio both dropped materially, so the branch should not change lines now. The remaining guardrail is still occupancy_limit_registers=1 with active warps stuck near 16.6, but the profile also shows compute-memory and DRAM throughput already much higher than before. That shifts ranking toward end-to-end PTX follow-through on export and full-width dataflow control, while explicitly avoiding the previously rejected explicit mma.sync half-panel subpath.
